LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT LC GENERAL ENGINEERING & CONSTRUCTION, INC., ARGONAUT INSURANCE COMPANY'S MOTION TO DISMISS SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT. THE COURT ADOPTS THE TENATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT LC GENERAL ENGINEERING & CONSTRUCTION, INC.'S MOTION TO DISMISS THE SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT FOR DELAY IN PROSECUTION IS DENIED. THE ORIGINAL COMPLAINT IN THIS CASE WAS FILED ON SEPTEMBER 6, 2016. PLAINTIFF SERVED LC GENERAL WITH THE COMPLAINT ON SEPTEMBER 30, 2016, LC GENERAL PARTICIPATED IN DISCOVERY IN THIS ACTION, AND ITS COUNSEL MET AND CONFERRED WITH PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL REGARDING THE CAUSES OF ACTION ALLEGED IN THE ORIGINAL COMPLAINT. (OLSEN DECL. 9-13 & EX. C; REPLY AT 2 ["BREEN DID SERVE THE COMPLAINT ON LCG AND RESPOND TO DISCOVERY"].) FOR REASONS THAT ARE NOT CLEAR TO THE COURT, HOWEVER, IT APPEARS THAT LC GENERAL NEVER FILED A RESPONSIVE PLEADING TO THE COMPLAINT OR THE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR FULL DETAILS)ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS) JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM, REPORTED. =(302/EPS)

DEFENDANT LC GENERAL ENGINEERING & CONSTRUCTION, INC.'S DEMURRER TO THE SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE FIRST AND THIRD CAUSES OF ACTION, AND IS OFF CALENDAR AS MOOT WITH RESPECT TO THE SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION. (SEE WORD MINUTES FOR FULL DETAILS) THE DEMURRER TO THE SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION FOR BAD FAITH DENIAL OF CLAIM ON SURETY BOND IS OFF CALENDAR AS MOOT. (SEE WORD MINUTES FOR FULL DETAILS) ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS) JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: M. calendar.matters:
Motion For Attorney Fees
Off Calendar Mar-09-2020 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Mark Meyer, Esq. of LOSCH EHRLICH & MEYER, T:415.956.8400, for defendant/moving party;
Howard Olsen, Esq. of the LAW OFFICE OF MICHAEL HEATH, T:415.931.4207, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. After argument, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT LC GENERAL ENGINEERING & CONSTRUCTION, INC., ARGONAUT INSURANCE COMPANY's Motion For Attorney Fees as follows:

Defendants LC General Engineering & Construction, Inc. and Argonaut Insurance Company's motion for attorney fees is granted in the amount of $21,160.00 in attorney's fees and $1,060.45 in costs. Plaintiff Tom Breen brought this action against Defendants LC General, the general contractor on three public works projects for the City and County of San Francisco, and Argonaut Insurance Company. Plaintiff alleged that Argonaut issued payment bonds on the projects to LC General, that he made written claims with Argonaut for payment on those bonds, and that Argonaut denied those claims. Plaintiff also alleged that LC General obtained a stop-notice release bond, and that LC General was the principal of the surety on that bond. In his second amended complaint, plaintiff sought to state claims against LC General and Argonaut for payment on stop-notice release bond and for bad faith denial of his claims on the payment bonds. Defendants are entitled to a mandatory award of attorney fees pursuant to Civil Code section 9564. Section 9564 is part of Title 3, "Public Work of Improvement," which generally provides that certain persons who have not been paid in full may give a stop payment notice to the public entity or assert a claim against a payment bond given by a direct contractor on a public works contract. (Civ. Code 9100(a), 9550(a).) The payment bond shall provide that "if the direct contractor or a subcontractor fails to pay a person authorized to assert a claim against a payment bond, "the surety will pay the obligation and, if an action is brought to enforce the liability on the bond, a reasonable attorney's fee, to be fixed by the court." (Id. 9554(b).) Further, a claimant "may maintain an action to enforce the liability of a surety on a payment bond whether or not the claimant has given the public entity a stop payment notice." (Id. 9564(a).) "In an action to enforce the liability on the bond, the court shall award the prevailing party a reasonable attorney's fee." (Id. 9564(c).) This is "an action to enforce the liability on the [payment] bond[s]" within the plain meaning of section 9564. Nothing in the plain language of section 9564 supports Plaintiff's argument that it authorizes fees awards only against sureties. Further, courts have rejected similar attempts to read the statute narrowly. In Liton General Engineering Contractor, Inc. v. United Pacific Insurance (1993) 16 Cal.App.4th 577, the court affirmed a judgment requiring a surety to pay attorney fees incurred by a subcontractor in its action on a public works payment bond, where the bulk of fees were incurred in arbitration of the subcontractor's contractual dispute with the general contractor, a proceeding to which the surety was not a party. The court rejected the contention that the statutory right to attorney fees under former section 3250 [now section 9564] applies only to actions on the bond to which the surety is a party. (Id. at 582, 585-587.)

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT LC GENERAL ENGINEERING & CONSTRUCTION, INC., ARGONAUT INSURANCE COMPANY's DEMURRER TO SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant LC General Engineering & Construction, Inc.'s demurrer to the second amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the first and third causes of action, and is off calendar as moot with respect to the second cause of action. The demurrer by Argonaut Insurance Company is off calendar in light of the court's ruling granting Argonaut's motion to dismiss for delay in prosecution. (see word minutes for full details)

Defendant Lc General Engineering & Construction, Inc., Argonaut Insurance Company Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (2 Of 2)
Off Calendar Sep-16-2019 Off Calendar

Defendants Lc General Engineering Construction, Inc. And Argonaut Insurance Company'S Notice Of Motion To Dismiss Second Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Sep-16-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Howard Olsen, Esq. 415-931-4207 of the Law Office of Michael Heath for Plaintiff.
Mark Meyer, Esq. 415-956-8400 of Losch & Ehrlich for Defendant LCG & Argonaut.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT LC GENERAL ENGINEERING & CONSTRUCTION, INC., ARGONAUT INSURANCE COMPANY'S Motion To Dismiss Second Amended Complaint.

The Court adopts the tenative ruling. Defendant LC General Engineering & Construction, Inc.'s motion to dismiss the second amended complaint for delay in prosecution is denied. The original complaint in this case was filed on September 6, 2016. Plaintiff served LC General with the complaint on September 30, 2016, LC General participated in discovery in this action, and its counsel met and conferred with plaintiff's counsel regarding the causes of action alleged in the original complaint. (Olsen Decl. 9-13 & Ex. C; Reply at 2 ["Breen did serve the complaint on LCG and respond to discovery"].) For reasons that are not clear to the Court, however, it appears that LC General never filed a responsive pleading to the complaint or the first amended complaint.

Defendant Argonaut Insurance Company's motion to dismiss is granted. Argonaut is not named as a party in the second amended complaint (or prior complaints), and it appears that plaintiff never sought leave to amend the complaint to name Argonaut as a Doe defendant. Nevertheless, the court's docket reflects that plaintiff first purported to serve Argonaut with the summons and second amended complaint on July 10, 2019.

The court has discretion to dismiss a complaint for delay in prosecution if service is not made within two years after the action is commenced against the defendant. (Code Civ. Proc. 583.420(a)(1).) The period runs from the commencement of the action, which means when the original complaint is filed. (Code Civ. Proc. 583.210(a), 411.10.) The rule applies even where the defendant seeking dismissal was served as a Doe defendant. (See Inversiones Papaluchi S.A.S. v. Superior Court (Robinson Helicopter Company, Inc.) 20 Cal.App.5th 1055, 1061 [cross-complaint dismissed where defendants not designated as Roes until almost three years later and not served until more than three years later; time for service ran from filing of original cross-complaint].) Here, defendants show that as early as January 2015, plaintiff was aware of Argonaut's identity as one of the two surety companies on the project. (Meyer Decl. 7.) Plaintiff concedes as much in his second amended complaint. (See SAC 35-36 [alleging that on January 23, 2015, Argonaut denied Breen's claim on payment bonds].) Moreover, plaintiff does not offer any excuse for his nearly three-year delay in serving Argonaut. "When the plaintiff has not served within the two-year period, the plaintiff is required to show excusable delay." (Terzian v. County of Ventura (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 78, 83; accord, Putnam v. Clague (1992) 3 Cal.App.4th 542, 549 ["'When faced with a motion to dismiss it is incumbent upon plaintiff to establish reasonable or excusable delay in service. If such a showing is not made, the court may dismiss the action in its discretion even if defendant fails to establish actual prejudice.'"].) Because plaintiff has not attempted to meet his burden, Argonaut's motion must be granted.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
